species.description: The ultimate speedster of the ocean, highly prized for sushi.

fishing.notes: Strict quotas and seasons apply. Mediterranean Bluefin is highly regulated.
fishing.regulations: ICCAT regulations apply. Catch and release is often mandatory for recreational anglers.

content.title: Mediterranean Giants: The Bluefin Migration
content.metaDescription: Chasing the world's most valuable fish in the ancient waters of the Mediterranean.
content.description: The Mediterranean Sea is the historic spawning ground for the Atlantic Bluefin Tuna.

editorial.introduction: For millennia, the arrival of the Bluefin Tuna in the Mediterranean has signaled the start of a legendary harvest. Known as the 'Mattanza' in Sicily, this ancient relationship between man and fish has evolved from traditional net traps to the modern pursuit of heavy-tackle sportfishing. Today, the bluefin remains the king of the Mediterranean, challenging anglers with its sheer power and relentless stamina.

quote.text: Fighting a 500lb Bluefin in the Mediterranean is like being hooked to a freight train that doesn't want to stop. It's a test of gear, physical endurance, and pure will.
quote.author: Capt. Lorenzo Rossi, Mediterranean Tuna Specialist

sections.heading: The Migration Route
sections.body:
Every spring, massive schools of Bluefin Tuna migrate from the cold waters of the Atlantic through the Straits of Gibraltar into the Mediterranean. They come to spawn in the warm, salt-rich waters off the coasts of Sicily, Sardinia, and the Balearic Islands.

For the sport angler, this migration creates a window of opportunity where some of the largest fish on earth are concentrated in relatively small areas. Drifting with live or chunked sardines is the most effective method used by local charters.
sections.caption: A school of Bluefin Tuna feeding near the surface during the spring migration.

sections.heading: Sustainability and Ethics
sections.body: Due to decades of overfishing, the Atlantic Bluefin is now a strictly managed species. The International Commission for the Conservation of Atlantic Tunas (ICCAT) sets rigorous quotas that change annually. Recreational anglers must adhere to 'No-Kill' policies in many zones, ensuring these magnificent creatures can return to their spawning grounds.
